speaker: interviewer
text: It's September 5, 1996. The survivor is Regina Horowitz, H-O-R-O-W-I-T-Z. The interviewer is Andrew Hartmann, H-A-R-T-M-A-N-N. We are in Tamarac, in the Miami, Florida District, USA. The language is English. And this is roll 1.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Andrew Hartmann, H-A-R-T-M-A-N-N. Today's date is September 5, 1996. I'm conducting an interview with Regina Horowitz, H-O-R-O-W-I-T-Z. We're in Tamarac, Florida in the USA. Please tell us your name and spell your last name.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Regina Horowitz, H-O-R-O-W-I-T-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Regina Wiener.

speaker: interviewer
text: And spell Wiener, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: W-I-E-N-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you ever have a nickname?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: August 13, 1923.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm now 73 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cracow, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Cracow, Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please tell me a little bit about your family. What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Baruch Wiener.

speaker: interviewer
text: Baruch Wiener.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Leah Wiener. And I had three older sisters. Their names were Mala, Pola, and Rachel. I was the youngest of the four. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I led a quite a happy childhood, as happy as it could be in Poland. But we did not know any better. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My father was an-- I would consider an Orthodox Jew but with a very open mind, open-minded person. He was a gentle, generous human being, and so was my mom. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was a difference of age between my sister, my older sister and myself, 11 years. She was a young lady when I was born. And but still, I grew up in a loving home, and it seemed to me that I have more than one mother around me. I was the youngest of the whole family's cousins. My mother was-- my mother had nine siblings. And I was the youngest person of all my cousins, the baby of the family, spoiled rotten. If I couldn't get something out of my parents, my uncles or my aunts produced it for me. One way or another, I had it. And if I did not like something, I stopped eating. I went on strike, eating. And that was the worst thing I could have done to my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was an accountant, what you call in America, accountant.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he work for himself, or did he work for a company?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was self-employed. He had a few [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] firms that he was their accountant. Took care on their books, took care on their taxes. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And he was self-employed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he attend synagogue regularly?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he was attending synagogue regular, on daily basis.

speaker: interviewer
text: Daily?

speaker: interviewee
text: On a daily basis, yes. And my grandmother, his mother, lived with us as long as I can remember. Because I never knew my grandfather, because I understand he passed away during influenza outbreak in 1917. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And being my father was the one and only child, naturally his mother remained with us. And as a matter of fact, my grandmother survived her one and only child and her daughter-in-law. And she survived two of her grandchildren during the war. He died-- she died on her own bed in her brother's house. She went to sleep.

speaker: interviewer
text: After the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, during the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: During the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: During the war. Her only prayers were not to die from a German-- the German should not kill her. She should not die from a German bullet. God was listen, listens to her. She was being envied by an awful lot of people in the ghetto A or B, whichever one the, the old people lived in, that she died, and she was not taken on a transport. She died in March of '43, before they made judenfrei the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: What, what was your mother's occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was a housewife. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she was a gentle lady, nice human being. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my sisters were-- as I was growing up, my sisters began to work as bookkeepers, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] secretaries. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we led a pretty decent life. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And when the war broke out in 1939, I had just graduated high school, June of '39. My life was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] how can I say it? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The war broke out at a time where I thought the days of my life was my oyster. But it was not meant to be. I never knew of hardships, but I learned it very fast. I grew up overnight. From a spoiled brat, I began to understand that it would be my duty, because my sisters were still employed-- my-- for the time being, and it was my duty not to allow my mother or my grandmother to go shopping for food. I was the one that had to learn how to buy, if there was a chicken to be bought, or where to go to scrounge around for bread for the family, or whatever else. I grew up very quickly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me a little bit about your life in school and your relationship, if any, with, with the Polish, non-Jewish children? When the war broke out, you were 16, I believe.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then how was your life in your early teens, in relation to the Poles when you were 12 and 13, 14?

speaker: interviewee
text: Early childhood. I, most of the time, in elementary school, I was mostly with Jewish children. In high school, it was like 50-50. We had Jewish and non-Jewish children alike. The school was open on Saturdays, the only time my father gave me permission to attend my school when I had-- once a month, when I had to make a book report, where I didn't use pencils or anything like that. I just went to school for that particular subject and gave my report orally and naturally. The paper I handed to the teacher. And that's the only time I was permitted to go on a Saturday to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- did you suffer any anti-Semitism from the Polish community, the children, as well as any teachers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not in our school. That was hand-picked people. Jewish and the non-Jewish children were hand-picked. You had to have an ability to-- for languages, and you had to have an ability for math.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH], language school.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. So you were picked for that?

speaker: interviewee
text: You were. Yes, you were hand-picked. Not every child that applied was accepted, being it was a private school. And you had to be a real conscientious student to be able to attend and to be able to take all that pressure of a daily routine. Because we had four languages on a daily basis, which was Polish, English, French, and German.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, then, you learned English, even in your early years in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: I learned, yes, the background. My, my English, I had a background of it. It came in quite handy. It took me a few, a few, few months before I-- after I came to this country to use my English. But it came very, very handy, the background, for my grammar and for my speaking. It took me a while to get adjusted to the-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] to the way I was taught the English expressions with the English pronunciation. Americans have a little-- it took me a while to get used to understand every word that was spoken by Americans. And now I think I'm pretty good on that. I had forgotten what I was taught. But God bless America. I'll say it a million one times in a day.

speaker: interviewer
text: So do I understand that, prior to 1939, you did not experience anti-Semitism from the schools?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Anti-Semitism in Poland, yes. But you asked me a question directly in school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, directly in school, I did not. Because those girls-- on the contrary. Some of the non-Jewish girls died in Montelupich. During the war, they-- two of them died in the prison on Montelupich by-- because they were the partisans. They were the anti-German. They were not anti-Jews. They were good citizen of Poland. They were good patriot of Poland. And one died, after the-- after the Germans-- the liberation, she died, because she contracted [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] lung, lung cancer and whatever in, in the prison. So I actually did not feel much of anti-Semitism from them directly. But anti-Semitism around me was always there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you describe it?

speaker: interviewee
text: The laws. First of all, the laws, our anti-Semitic laws, like [NON-ENGLISH]. This was a priest who was a senator. And Mrs. [? Pristerova. ?] They instilled in the '30s that there will be no kosher handling of the animals to slaughter. They installed the anti-Jewish money laws, which were introduced to the Polish public. But nothing to compare with what was with the Nazis. That really did not compare. We still were allowed to live like people. And we still were allowed to keep our-- whatever we owned. We still were allowed to, to have that. I mean, as I was growing up, these things weren't as good as we thought it might be, but we lived. I recall, and I don't remember exactly if it was just before the war in '39 or a year prior to that, when the slaughterhouse were closed for Jews. The Jews went out on strike. And you know, in our history, we have these three weeks between one fast and Tisha B'av. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We did not, by any means, we did not buy any chickens. And the Jews in Cracow did not eat meat, strictly dairy. And the, the Polish-- that was must have been a year prior to the outbreak of the war, because the Polish population themselves objected to that. Because the farmers were bringing chickens and what to the market, and they had no-- there was no, there were no sales. So they did not like that idea. And somehow it was re-voted for a, for a, for a little while. But it was not as good as it used to be, like my mom used to tell me years ago. Maybe I did not remember as well. Anti-Semitism was in Poland, rampant. I will not deny that, especially at the universities and in higher education schools. A Jewish child was not allowed to enter a gymnasium or a lyceum. Or even the, the university was very tough for a Jewish person to enter. I know a few people-- I knew a few people who had to leave the country. And when the war broke out, they were either in Italy or Paris or France. And they-- some of them perished from there. But some of them survived, because they had an opportunity to leave, be it for Cuba or for Dominican Republic, or even some of them had relatives in the United States that were able to come. But their members of their families that remained in Poland perished. I met some of the-- someone-- all right, unfortunately that person is dead by now. But I knew them in, in New York, when I-- when we came to New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your family able to celebrate the Jewish holidays and festivals?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we did. Yes, we celebrated. But I'm speaking of before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We did celebrate. There was always some incidents here or there, but we were quite aware of this. And we tried not to go out many at once together. We tried to scatter somehow, you know, and to empty out the temple, the synagogues, slowly, not everybody at once, like en masse. But we did. We did. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And... But nothing to compare of what transpired after the Nazis took over.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we go on, I just want to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] ask. When you said there were some incidents that you know of, personally-- and would you describe them? Or that you heard about?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not me, personally.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not me personal, and fortunately, not my family directly. But here and there that we heard that somebody was-- that a stone was thrown at someone, or something like that, that they called us hooligans. It was not the stage to do it, but just somebody had it in for someone or whatever. So they threw stones. They threw rocks or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you carry identification papers that marked you to be Jewish or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not on the Polish government, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we continue and go on to when the war began, is there anything about the life before the war that I didn't ask you that you want to add before we go to the next area?

speaker: interviewee
text: Look, whoever was able to used to go on vacations, had vacation-- we had beautiful vacation places in the Carpathian Mountains. Later, some people used to go to the sea, to the Baltic Sea. It depends the area where you lived, and it depended where you-- how much you could afford.

speaker: interviewer
text: But some people did participate in these things. Yes, to be alive was, how can I say? In this country, not everybody can afford vacations, either. But some do, and some don't. So the same was more or less-- it's relevant. The same was there, too. But I could say-- I could picture-- I could say that my life was pretty stable as far as education was concerned and as far as even going on a vacation.

speaker: interviewee
text: As a matter of fact, the summer of '39, I went to camp in Jordanow. And we heard-- toward the end of the, the last week, we began to hear shooting, like on the border. Because it was not too far from between the Slovak-- the Slovakian, Polish, and German border was in that part of the country. And across the mountains, the Tatra Mountains, was Czechoslovakia, actually. And we heard, sometimes during the night, or even during the day, but we thought that was thunder. But it was gun.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the place, Jordanow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jordanow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you mind spelling that?

speaker: interviewee
text: J-O-R-D-A-N-O-W.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a little village in the mountain area. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I never retrieved my luggage. I finally was able to reach my home, my hometown, Cracow, under great difficulties. Because it was a camp for girls and youngsters, and I was one of the older children then. And it was very hard to get onto the train, because they were transporting the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] army people to and fro. And it was very hard-- and besides, it was very hard. People were running. They did not know where to. But many more people were running. We could hardly make it to Cracow. A trip that was, under normal conditions on that train, took three to four hours. It took over a night.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now you're talking about August 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had just turned 16?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yeah. And I final-- we, we got-- we reached our-- I reached my home towards, I think, August 30. But we started our trek-- we walked to the station instead of having transportation, because there was none anymore. And then it was very hard to get on that-- on the train. We held on for dear life. Standing room was like sardines, but we finally made it. And I arrived home with whatever I had on my back. And the luggage already we could not retrieve. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And a day or two later, the war broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you when you heard that the war had started?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- I recall it was 7 o'clock in the morning. At 6 o'clock, a bang woke us all up. My father was already on the way to shul. He came back, and he said that the war broke out, that the Germans bombed the station in Cracow. We lived quite far away from the station. But we heard the bang. And that was the one and only time that Cracow was bombarded.

speaker: interviewer
text: What street did you live on in Cracow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dietlowska.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you mind spelling that?

speaker: interviewee
text: D-I-E-T-L-O-W-S-K-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that in the-- what was known as the Jewish quarter of Cracow?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the outskirts of the Jewish quarter.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was an area, I don't know if you've heard of [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Um-hum.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was [NON-ENGLISH], arena, where the ball games took place. We lived right across the street from that. As a matter of fact, you wanted-- if we wanted to watch them, we were going to our neighbor's home on the third floor or the second floor, and we could watch from our, from our building.

speaker: interviewer
text: This reminds me of a question. Did you participate-- since you were a teenager at the age of 14, 15, 16-- did you participate in any Zionist organization such as--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not as a youngster, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Hashomer or any such an organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not as a youngster, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was too busy with, with whatever, growing up, I guess, and school work--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Please continue at the point where we had talked about, when you heard a loud bang, when the war started. Your father came home from shul.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father came home, and he said the war broke out. Whatever the papers were writing, it's here already. Now what do we do? Now, my father went on-- that was on a Friday. The first was on a Friday. On Sunday, a lot of people walked from different little cities, little towns, and they were passing through Cracow. And they were saying, we're walking. We don't know where, but we're walking. And if you're smart enough, you'll walk, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, by Monday morning when my father came from the shul, he said a lot of young people are going. I want you girls to go. Says where to? What I-- he says, I cannot walk that much. My wife certainly-- that means my mother-- cannot do it. Her feet are too bad. And my mother, what am I going-- with my grandmother-- what am I going to do? I have a, a mother and a wife to, to support and to stay with. But you are unattached. You are young, then go.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, our decision was not to go, to stay with our parents. Give my father the same reasoning he gave us, that you are our parents, and we're staying here. And we did. Right or wrong, I'm not questioning that. That was our decision at that time. And I never-- I'm going to skip a little while, something, and I'm-- as we are on that subject.

speaker: interviewer
text: When my daughter came home, and when she opened up the letter of acceptance at the University of Minnesota for her graduate work, and she was so excited. And without even asking, she ran to the phone and called up and said, I'm accepting it. And after she hung up, I said to her, how come she didn't even ask? Ma, that's my lifetime dream. When my husband came from work, he was very upset at her and about it. And I looked at him, and I said, you know what? We have to let go our children. Let's not repeat what happened in my family. Let's not repeat that. We have to let her go. We had to let her grow up. I said, you left your family, too. We have to let go. And he calmed down a little bit.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, going back to our lives in Europe and the Nazis. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The Nazis came to Cracow on the 6th of, of-- September 6. And right away, before I-- it seems to me they must have had either a printer with them or come right prepared with all the edicts that were put up all over the place, on the buildings, on, on, on trees, wherever it was. Edict the Jew, that is a curfew for the Jew. Jews are not allowed after 6:30 on the streets. And Jews are not allowed congregate. Jews are not allowed to worship together. Jews are not allowed an awful lot of things. That was the first day. The second day, they took hostages, then prominent Jewish leaders of the city. They took them as hostages, because they gave us an ultimatum. Cracow was, is built in a, in a circle, like, like a shoe, horseshoe. It's a very beautiful city, but it's surrounded also with a, with a lot of greenery. The old city has one layer. The outer city has another layer of parks. Instead of walking through streets, you can walk through the parks and wind up in the same place, instead of walking through the crowds. And all these areas had the-- had the Polish army was making dugouts in these areas. And the edict was given that the Jews have to come out en masse and close up those dugouts, the, the trenches. And they gave, I don't recall exactly, how much time to do it, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went. I, I never had a shovel, never mind a heavy one. I never had one in my hand. But I worked one day. We didn't finish.

speaker: interviewer
text: We worked another day. Then Yom Kippur came around. And the, the Jewish police knocked on the door, would you mind? Come, people, come out, let's go. So I volunteered my services. As I said, I grew up very quickly. And the whole day on Yom Kippur, we stood there, and we were closing up whatever it was. And the Polish people came out with water and with such venom. [NON-ENGLISH]. Only Hitler could help [? your-- ?] you lousy Jews. You want water? Here, spilt on us. Those are the nice times in my life. We're going to stop for a moment to change the tape.
